story.title: Map of the world's great castles and fortresses

comments.textPlain: Hi thanks for the feedback and thank you Marklit to submit it here!Thecastlemap.com is one of the websites I operate. I also operate trainrouter.com, sunshineatlas.com. and europebeachmap.com.Honestly, I did not want to focus too much on this website due to what I thought of a market of low demand. However, there are so many critical comments. Hence, I will fix it properly and this will be one of the websites I will fully commit to.I appreciate everything guys even if you probably do not like the website. The reason why I made the website is because I reallly like castles....
I love that the annual snow castle in Kemi, Finland, is included =D https://thecastlemap.com/castles/snowcastle-of-kemi/

Cool idea, horrible dataset. Its limited to things in Wikidata rather than using OSM, and it must have an article on Wikipedia in English to count so I am sure it is missing just based on that. And somehow it misses many castles (and other categories it includes) which do meet those requirements.There is no definition of what they use to categorize anything either. And it clearly isn't applied consistently. There is a Château category and yet many things with Château in their name are tagged as castles.

Interesting but very difficult to perform in the right way. Example: Italy has maybe 45k buildings that could show on that map https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Categoria:Castelli_d%27Italia
but they can't be collected by looking at the English Wikipedia so it's almost empty compared to what could be. Other countries, same problem.Somebody suggested in other comments that the data could be from OpenStreetMap. Or let people add their own castles, but then you have all the sort of problems of content moderation.
Also found thishttps://castles.tello.app

I am working on fixing the site. It is vibe coded which I understand is a problem. Ai took my career path so I have use it as well although I fucking hate AI. I like creating stuff though.I have only used English Wikipedia for putting castles on the map. To some degrees using Wikipedia of other languages will fix the problem partially (Which some of you have suggested). The ultimate fix would be a community who does all the suggestions, because all datasets are wrong anyways.The claim that France does have 45k castles is just so wrong lol. Yes the site does miss many castles and some are defined wrong. I am working on that and I am really thankful for all the suggestions which will be credited in the changelog: https://thecastlemap.com/changelog/Anyways I am working on the site and updating it rather quickly because I never thought people wanted a castle site.
This is off by many degrees of magnitude for Ireland at least: https://www.reddit.com/r/dataisbeautiful/comments/1nkvqd7/oc...

Nice project :3There's a MASSIVE amount of castles missingJust for France, the castle visible on the map -> this is not even 5%Depending on the count it goes from 20000 castles to 45000 (just for France!)

It’s cool. One issue I have is that the colours for “Castle & château” and “Ruin” are very similar.When you tap a marker often you can’t see the castle in the photo because it’s cropped to fit a rectangle with an unusual aspect ratio. That element should be allowed to adjust its height so the image is scaled at its own aspect ratio rather than cropped. Eg you can only see the mounds of Berwick Castle and Bamburgh Castle in the North East of England.Also this one should be included: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Caisteal_Maol
Clearly vibe-coded. Cool idea though. I can see what was leveraged from the Wikipedia data set.Side question: why does Claude prefer dark backgrounds with low-contrast text?

I had to look up Madagascar's castle: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rova_of_AntananarivoThe Rova of Antananarivo /ˈruːvə/ (Malagasy: Rovan'i Manjakamiadana [ˈruvᵊn manˌdzakəmiˈadə̥nᵊ]) is a royal palace complex (rova) in Madagascar that was the home of the sovereigns of the Kingdom of Imerina in the 17th and 18th centuries, as well as of the rulers of the Kingdom of Madagascar in the 19th century. Located in the central highland city of Antananarivo, the Rova occupies the highest point on Analamanga, formerly the highest of Antananarivo's many hills.
Very cool project, but obviously missing (too) many great castles. I'm not sure if that way of measuring popularity is really well done.

Not sure if they count, but the site appears to be missing loads of British star-forts built around the great lakes, st. lawrence, and eastern Canada!https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Citadel_Hill_(Fort_George)https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Georges_Island_(Nova_Scotia)https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Citadelle_of_Quebechttps://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Old_Fort_Eriehttps://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fort_AnneThey built these things _everywhere_, probably a good part of what bankrupted the British Empire!https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fort_Charlotte_(Nassau)https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prince_of_Wales_Fort

Nice project, but what is "hidden" gem about Elmina castle in Ghana?If Cape Coast Castle is described as "world renowned", then Elmina should be as well. I live in Ghana their popularity is almost evenly matched.Also Elmina castle is the oldest and largest European building in existence in sub-Saharan Africa.One more thing, it is kind of hard to click on specific dots when they are so clustered together. I had to zoom in a lot to see and be able to click on some of the castles I knew were there.
My first dotcom began by listing German castles for sale after the wall fell. https://web.archive.org/web/19970102101502/http://www.re.com...

First, hat tip for this gorgeous website.  Very well made.  The UX is lovely.Looking at Tokyo, Japan, I see that Hama-rikyū Gardens is included.  Wiki says:    > The main palace at Hama was destroyed by fire in 1724, and has not been rebuilt since.

Ref: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hama-riky%C5%AB_GardensAs I understand, it is a beautiful garden, not a castle.

Palácio da Bolsa in Porto is not, at all, a castle. It was built in the XIX century as the stock market and never served defensive purposes as castles would. Not to speak about the sheer amount of misses in Portugal. This is off by a lot!

Comment from dev in previous submission:>This project is in early stages and the information is brought by wikipedia. The goal of this project is to make castle viewing a bit more fun.https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48861309
I think it's cool. Having lived in Jordan for a while I see there are some missing castles there. I will look into adding them in Wikidata.

The North American bits are a little weird. Colorado has an entry for Bent's Fort but not for Fort Vasquez, though both are rebuilt tourist attractions. Then there's Bishop's Castle, an actual norman style castle built outside of Pueblo by some crazed guy
This dataset is bad. The North Carolina state capitol is a castle?
Why nothing in North America but forts? There are lots of ancient structures that should be considered. Ex: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Montezuma_Castle_National_Monu...
very cool. some feedback: tricky to mouse over the tooltip to click links within the tooltip
What's really neat about this isn't just the visualization, but that they’ve packaged the whole dataset as open GeoJSON/CSV mirrored on Hugging Face and GitHub—plus an MCP endpoint for AI agents.
That's less than I expected for worldwideApparently Russia, China, Kazakhstan, Mongolia, etc... were not really into castles, while Japan was?

I went to a castle in Dunedin, New Zealand, but NZ seems to be castle-less: Larnach Castle, https://www.larnachcastle.co.nz/.Brazil also has a few castles, although not sure if they are classified differently -- https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lista_de_castelos_no_Brasil

The local "castle" on the map is Fort Duquesne, which is listed as "ruins" but in fact has no ruins to visit. It says "The site of both forts is now occupied by Point State Park, where the outlines of the two forts have been laid in granite slabs" - which is true, but...there are no ruins here!If forts count as castles, at least do Fort Pitt, which has the blockhouse functional.Anyways - this is a fun project and it should continue!

Needs some of the ones referenced here (including the Dupont Castle, the work of the site's author, who lasted provided a work update in '22): https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=27929729See also https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=44948352
Wrong title. Wrong not even by two orders of magnitude.
There's many more castles in Ireland than you showed. Orgs like the Office of Public Works would have a list.
Is this anything more substantial than a weekend-coded public data scrape?

Is Fort Ross really "Renowned" as listed? I've never heard of it and honestly it doesn't look very interesting. I'd rather visit Fort Knox which is a real fortification currently in use, world famous, and not listed here.
Clicking on full story doesn't do anything. It closes the popup (mobile android)
This is not a "map of castles".I checked my local area, and it omits interesting castles, instead including palaces, landmarks and world war era fortresses.Shameful submission.
The ones in the area where I used to live are all missing.
We just stayed in Dalhousie Castle, the oldest in Scotland. Very beautiful and well maintained, with lots of spooky history.If you want to leave a legacy, build something out of stone.
Excellent usecase of map and history. Request to add a year range selector or slider
Italy, like Germany, Austria and maybe a few more European countries host more than 5,000 "castles".
Since forts are included in the USA, there are loads of Napoleonic forts missing in the UK.Plymouth is surrounded by them.
